data.title: Janus Henderson Mortgage-Backed Securities ETF Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

The U.S. MBS market posted a negative quarterly gain, with the Bloomberg US MBS Index falling 3.16%. The U.S. economy remained resilient, as third quarter 2024 gross domestic product (GDP) growth came in at 2.8% and the unemployment rate held steady around 4.1%. While Treasury markets sold off, risk markets reacted favorably to the decisive Republican Party victory in the November election, due to expectations for expansionary fiscal policies and deregulation. However, after starting the rate-cutting cycle with a 50- basis-point (bps) cut in September, the Federal Reserve (Fed) took a decidedly more hawkish tone at its December meeting, with the revised Fed dot plot implying just 50 bps of rate cuts in 2025. The yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) ended the quarter at 4.57% relative to 3.78% at the end of September. Spreads on agency MBS ended virtually unchanged at 43 bps. The rate on a 30-year conventional mortgage ended higher at 7.28%.

Portfolio review

MBS returns were adversely affected by rising Treasury rates following the November election. Rates markets backed up to account for a potentially slower pace of rate cuts amid the incoming administration’s pro-growth policies.

The portfolio’s longer duration position detracted as rates rose, but we remain positioned for the likely re-steepening of the yield curve.

Security selection within agency mortgage-backed securities contributed, as did an allocation to non-agency mortgages. We increased our allocation to agency floating rate securities to around 10% of the portfolio as we saw the opportunity to capture a high level of coupon income with much lower prepayment risk.

While we believe the MBS asset class is very attractively priced relative to other fixed income sectors, there is significant dispersion among various pools of MBS. Some pools exhibit high prepayment risk, such as recently issued to-be-announced (TBA) mortgages with coupons in the 6%-8% range. In contrast to these TBA securities, we continue to focus on borrower stories within specified mortgage pools, as we seek to acquire mortgages offering attractive yields without elevated levels of prepayment risk.

Manager outlook

As we enter 2025, we believe investors should seek to take advantage of the positive drivers impacting MBS markets: the Fed having initiated its rate-cutting cycle, the likely normalization of rate-spread correlations due to lower inflation, and the defensive characteristics of MBS.

Now that the Fed has begun its rate-cutting cycle and rates markets have priced in a slower pace of cuts, we believe investors in MBS may lean into the yields offered and be less concerned with fighting rising rates. Even if inflation proves to be stickier due to the incoming administration’s policies, we think much of this risk is already priced in.

Real-time data indicates that inflation has continued to moderate toward the central bank’s target of 2%, and we believe that a resurgence of inflation above 3% is unlikely. Rates and spreads have historically been negatively correlated when inflation is below 3% – resulting in reduced portfolio volatility, as any material widening in spreads may be somewhat counterbalanced by falling rates.

The market is currently pricing in a terminal rate around 4%, which is notably higher than the Fed’s estimate of 3.1%. We expect the actual terminal rate to settle at a level lower than 4%, but we also expect the Fed’s estimate to move higher.

Historically, MBS has acted as a ballast during bouts of market volatility, making it a suitable defensive diversifier in multi-asset portfolios, in our view. And just as MBS were more adversely affected by rising rates and high interestrate volatility in the preceding few years, we anticipate that this trend reversing may provide an additional tailwind for returns going forward.

Portfolio management

John Kerschner, CFANick Childs, CFA

Definitions

Basis point (BP) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%.

Credit quality ratings are measured on a scale that generally ranges from AAA (highest) to D (lowest).

Credit spread is the difference in yield between securities with similar maturity but different credit quality. Widening spreads generally indicate deteriorating creditworthiness of corporate borrowers, and narrowing indicate improving.

Duration measures a bond price’s sensitivity to changes in interest rates. The longer a bond’s duration, the higher its sensitivity to changes in interest rates and vice versa. A yield curve plots the yields (interest rate) of bonds with equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Typically bonds with longer maturities have higher yields.

10-Year Treasury Yield is the interest rate on U.S. Treasury bonds that will mature 10 years from the date of purchase.

Quantitative Easing (QE) is a government monetary policy occasionally used to increase the money supply by buying government securities or other securities from the market. Monetary tightening refers to central bank activity aimed at curbing inflation and slowing down growth in the economy by raising interest rates and reducing the supply of money.

Please consider the charges, risks, expenses and investment objectives carefully before investing. For a prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus containing this and other information, please call Janus Henderson at 800.668.0434 or download the file from Products - US Advisor. Read it carefully before you invest or send money.

Returns include reinvestment of dividends and capital gains.

OBJECTIVE: Janus Henderson Mortgage-Backed Securities ETF (NYSEARCA:JMBS) seeks a high level of total return consisting of income and capital appreciation.

The opinions are as of 12/31/24, are subject to change and may not reflect the views of others in the organization. Janus Henderson may have a business relationship with certain entities discussed. The comments should not be construed as a recommendation of individual holdings or market sectors, but as an illustration of broader themes.

Holdings are subject to change without notice.

Performance attribution is a method used to analyze and quantify the performance of an investment portfolio relative to its benchmark and does not represent actual performance. Attribution is calculated using gross returns before the deduction of advisory fees and considers differences in weighting for the portfolio and the index. This analysis aims to break down the portfolio's performance into different components to understand what factors contributed to relative return, among other performance characteristics. The analysis can provide insights into which decisions positively or negatively impacted the portfolio's performance and serves as the basis for this discussion.

There is no assurance the stated objective(s) will be met.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal and fluctuation of value.

The Fund will typically enter into“to be announced” or “TBA” commitments when purchasing MBS, which allows the Fund to agree to pay for certain yet-to-be issued securities at a future date and which may have a leveraging effect on the Fund.

The Fund may enter into reverse repurchase agreement transactions and use the cash made available from these transactions to make additional investments in mortgage-related instruments or other fixed-income securities.

Fixed income securities are subject to interest rate, inflation, credit and default risk. The bond market is volatile. As interest rates rise, bond prices usually fall, and vice versa. The return of principal is not guaranteed, and prices may decline if an issuer fails to make timely payments or its credit strength weakens.

Mortgage-backed securities (MBS) may be more sensitive to interest rate changes. They are subject to extension risk, where borrowers extend the duration of their mortgages as interest rates rise, and prepayment risk, where borrowers pay off their mortgages earlier as interest rates fall. These risks may reduce returns.

Derivatives can be more volatile and sensitive to economic or market changes than other investments, which could result in losses exceeding the original investment and magnified by leverage.

Concentrated investments in a single sector, industry or region will be more susceptible to factors affecting that group and may be more volatile than less concentrated investments or the market as a whole.

Increased portfolio turnover may result in higher expenses and potentially higher net taxable gains or losses.

Actively managed portfolios may fail to produce the intended results. No investment strategy can ensure a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Bloomberg U.S. Mortgage Backed Securities (MBS) Index measures the performance of U.S. fixed-rate agency mortgage backed pass-through securities. Index performance does not reflect the expenses of managing a portfolio as an index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

Janus Henderson Investors US LLC is the investment adviser and ALPS Distributors, Inc. is the distributor. ALPS is not affiliated with Janus Henderson or any of its subsidiaries. Janus Henderson is a trademark of Janus Henderson Group plc or one of its subsidiaries. © Janus Henderson Group plc.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tesla's Roadmap: Key Catalysts And Growth Challenges
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Following a strong rally driven by Trump’s election—bolstering optimism around deregulation, tax incentives, and a pro-Elon Musk business environment, Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) has since underperformed the broader market. While my previous coverage focused on Tesla’s expansion into affordable models, energy deployments, and robotaxis, Q4 2024 results necessitate a reassessment of its fundamentals.

Top-line growth persists with strong energy deployments and expanding Full Self-Driving (FSD) subscriptions, but auto margins remain pressured by price cuts and rising Chinese competition. Political tailwinds may aid Tesla, yet EV market share risks grow. While Robotaxis offer long-term potential, execution hurdles persist. Therefore, Tesla’s trajectory depends on balancing affordability, sustaining FSD monetization, and managing competition, making it a long-term AI mobility play with execution risks.

Top-Line Growth in Q4-25 Amidst Expansion And FSD Monetization

Focusing on the revenue, Tesla’s Q4-24 top-line of $25.71B (missing estimates by $1.42B) represents a 2% YOY increase. This growth was modest but marked several core strengths. Firstly, the company hit record vehicle deliveries and energy storage deployments in Q4. The introduction of the New Model Y across all markets may support this momentum. Additionally, Tesla’s energy business hit its highest-ever gross profit in Q4, based on record Powerwall deployments and the completion of Megafactory Shanghai. Therefore, this will ramp up production and boost gross margin in the coming quarters.

Looking forward, Tesla’s consensus top-line estimates for upcoming years suggest an upward trend. The estimates for Q1-25 are $23.98B, representing 12.56% YOY growth. This growth may continue with Q2-25 top-line projected at $27.28B (+6.98% YOY a de-accelerated) and Q3-25 at $28.49B (+13.15% YOY), and Q4-25 at $30.41B (+18.28% YOY). These projections indicate a two-digit growth in CY25 fueled by more vehicle deliveries, new-market penetration, and new product launches. Further, company investments in infrastructure can continue to power this expansion in the medium to long term. The investments focus on vehicle manufacturing capacity for new models, AI training compute, and energy storage manufacturing capacity.

Strategically, Tesla is focusing on expanding its global footprint by looking at its record deliveries in China. Here, the Model Y is also the best-selling vehicle in FY24. In addition, Tesla’s penetration in new geographies like the Philippines and its high sales in South Korea and Europe (where the Model Y was already a leader in many countries) also point towards its global top-line growth.

Technically, Tesla’s innovations in FSD allow for a significant revenue gain via adding value to its vehicles. The company’s Q4 Vehicle Safety continued its YOY improvement in safety for vehicles equipped with FSD. There are vital enhancements in Version 13 and incremental updates leading to Version 14. The launch of the Cortex training cluster at Gigafactory Austin has been a vital instance of FSD advancement. In short, Tesla may continue to invest in training infrastructure to support both vehicle autonomy and the development of the Optimus humanoid robot in CY25.

Following Musk’s estimates, autonomous vehicles could increase the utility of a passenger car from ~10 hours per week to around 50–55 hours per week. This represents a 5X increase in asset utility. This increase in utility can be a massive top-line opportunity through ride-hailing services, cargo delivery, and other autonomous vehicle applications. Tesla plans to launch unsupervised FSD as a paid service in Austin in June 2025. The initial deployments are already underway at its Fremont factory. This move represents a sharp step towards monetizing Tesla’s autonomous driving tech and can boost the company’s top line as a result.

Now moving on to Tesla’s manufacturing capacity, the company is preparing its existing factories for the launch of new products in CY25 that include more affordable models. The New Model Y has already launched with a production ramp, and deliveries may begin later in Q1-25.

We will be introducing several new products throughout 2025. We are still on track to launch a more affordable model in the first half of 2025 and will continue to expand our lineup from there.

Vaibhav Taneja (Q4 2024 Earnings Call, emphasis added).

Additionally, Tesla is making progress in its Semi factory. Here, the first truck builds are scheduled to start by the end of CY25, and volume production is planned for early CY26. The company is preparing for the production of the Cybercab, which is scheduled for volume production starting in CY26. Tesla’s approach to manufacturing new vehicles on the same lines as its current models allows for more capital-efficient growth. This enables the company to fully utilize its current expected maximum capacity of close to three million vehicles, representing >60% growth over 2024 production levels. By leveraging existing manufacturing infrastructure, Tesla can hit high-volume growth without the need for substantial new CapEx.

Finally, Tesla’s current price-to-sales (P/S) ratio of 12.96 (TTM) is now around its long-term average (5Y) of 12.74. This indicates that the market value of TSLA is well below its price level under max street optimism. This undervaluation, considering Tesla’s stable market lead and tech potential, marks a good time to have a long position on the stock. Tech potential means the company’s focus on autonomy, energy storage, and new product launches pushes it to capitalize on emerging trends in the automotive and energy segments.

Revenue Miss Signals Broader Growth, Political, and Market Share Issues

Tesla’s Q4-24 performance points to a concerning trend of revenue misses and may signal weakness in top-line growth. In Q4-24, Tesla had actual revenue of $25.71B, falling short of the estimated $27.13B. It is a 5.25% surprise for now. This follows a similar pattern in Q3-24, where the actual top-line of $25.18B missed the $25.44B estimate by $259.57M, a -1.02% surprise. While the magnitude of the miss in FQ3 was smaller, the constant underperformance led to a complete flip for revenue % surprise in just 4 years, and it suggests a systemic issue. The revenue misses are troubling as the street historically focused on Tesla’s strong growth narrative. In short, the company has long been viewed as a high-growth disruptor in EV (automotive) and energy deployments. These negative results indicate that street expectations may disappear soon. If revenue misses do not stop, it can lead to a solid downside reevaluation of Tesla’s growth potential and its stock price.

This is already happening with the downward trend in revenue estimate revisions. Over the last three months, there have been 12 downward revisions to Tesla’s top-line estimates for the upcoming quarter, against only 2 upward revisions. This 6:1 ratio of downward to up revisions shows that analysts are increasingly pessimistic. Ford (F), General Motors (GM), Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY), and Chinese brands have been spending heavily on their EV models. As a result, they are hitting hard on Tesla’s market share. Additionally, Tesla’s reliance on a limited number of models (Model 3 and Model Y) may be making it more vulnerable.

Moreover, Tesla’s mega plans for FSD and autonomous vehicles also present significant issues. During the Q4-24 call, Musk had high confidence in the release of unsupervised FSD in California (Home of Democrats) and other regions by the end of the year. However, he also acknowledged the high safety standards required for such a release, noting that any accident involving an autonomous vehicle would attract significant media and Democrats’ attention. Even if the tech is statistically safer than human drivers, it can face heavy regulatory issues, as the Democrats are not feeling good about Musk and Trump. Interestingly, Tesla’s progress in this area has been slower than initially anticipated. The company has missed its self-imposed deadlines for achieving full autonomy back-to-back.

I mean, for example, for us to just to release supervised Full Self Driving in Europe, even though it works really well, we have to go through a mountain of paperwork with the Netherlands, which is our primary regulatory authority.

Elon Musk (Q4-24 Earnings Call.)

So, there is no guarantee that it will be able to overcome the remaining technical issues in the near future. It can become more difficult from a regulatory perspective as Democrats are now identified as anti-Musk. Similarly, Europe has a complex regulatory environment that could delay the rollout of unsupervised FSD. Tesla has to pass a “mountain of paperwork” with the Netherlands. The country serves as its primary regulatory authority in Europe before Tesla can even begin the process of obtaining EU-wide approval. This regulatory complexity can delay the commercialization of FSD in the core markets. In short, any setbacks in its FSD development and deployment may delay the revenue milestones and hit hard on the stock.

Takeaway

Tesla’s post-election rally has faded, with Q4 2024 results highlighting both growth opportunities and ongoing challenges. While energy deployments and FSD subscriptions continue to expand, auto margins remain under pressure due to price cuts and increasing competition. Political tailwinds may support Tesla, but market share risks persist. The success of Robotaxis and FSD monetization will be instrumental in making Tesla’s long-term vision of AI mobility a reality. However, execution risk, regulatory issues, and revenue shortfalls raise questions over near-term performance, making Tesla a high-reward, high-risk stock at its current valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Janus Henderson Mid Cap Value Managed Account Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

Mid-cap value stocks saw modest declines for the quarter, underperforming growth equities. After facing headwinds in October, stocks rallied in November as investors anticipated pro-business policies under President-elect Donald Trump. The resulting rally favored speculative and even unprofitable companies. Stocks experienced downward volatility in December as investors started to worry about the economic implications of some proposed Trump policies, such as tariffs. The Federal Reserve (Fed) cut policy rates by 25 basis points but signaled fewer rate cuts were likely in 2025. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) ended the quarter sharply higher on the inflationary outlook and forecast for fewer Fed rate cuts.

Portfolio review

While our focus on bottom-up stock selection and high-quality companies helped us outperform over calendar year 2024, we underperformed in a quarter characterized by speculative investor behavior. Concern over the impact of rising interest rates on economic growth also pressured several cyclical holdings, including materials company Freeport-McMoRan (FCX). The copper and gold producer struggled against a backdrop of weaker copper prices and slower economic growth in China. Westlake (WLK), a low-cost supplier of petrochemicals, including PVC pipe, and residential building products, detracted on a relative basis as its outlook has been clouded by cyclical concerns and fears that rising mortgage rates may derail an anticipated rebound in home sales and construction. These concerns also weighed on share price performance for Fortune Brands (FBIN), a supplier of doors, plumbing fixtures, and other housing-related accessories.

Stock selection in the utilities sector lifted relative performance, driven by strong share price performance for Entergy (ETR), an integrated power supplier in the South. Entergy delivered better-than-expected earnings growth and forecast increased electricity demand from data centers to meet the needs of artificial intelligence. In December, META announced it had selected Entergy to power a large data center complex it is developing in Louisiana.

Natural gas producer Expand Energy (EXE, formerly Chesapeake Energy) was another positive contributor, as firming natural gas prices supported its earnings outlook. We believe natural gas producers may also benefit when the incoming administration lifts restrictions on new liquified natural gas facilities.

The portfolio ended the quarter overweight in the industrials, materials, healthcare, energy, and consumer staples sectors. It was underweight in the information technology, real estate, utilities, financials, communication services, and consumer discretionary sectors.

Manager outlook

For 2025, we remain confident in our disciplined value investment approach, even as we see potential sources of market volatility. While the broader economy continues to grow, we have seen rolling recessions depressing activity in certain sectors, which may continue in 2025. Heading into the fourth quarter, investors hoped falling interest rates would provide relief for the housing market, consumer spending, and corporate balance sheets. Unfortunately, these hopes appear to have been dashed in the short term, with yields moving higher while the equity markets have not flinched. We see the potential for heightened volatility around potential shifts in public policy. Some Trump proposals, such as a continuation of current corporate tax rates, deregulation, and a more accommodating environment for mergers and acquisitions, would be positive for the kind of high-quality, profitable small- and mid-cap companies in which we invest. Other proposed policies, such as tariffs, could accelerate inflation or weigh on corporate profits. In our view, the prospect of higher inflation and higher-for-longer interest rates is not fully priced into the equity market and could set the stage for periods of turbulence in the new year.

We remain alert to imbalances we have seen in the broader equity market, where mega-cap and AI-related stocks have overshadowed compelling investments in the small- and mid-cap arena. It is important to remain vigilant given the potential “unwinding” from this narrow market, though this shift could be beneficial for smaller-cap companies trading at significant discounts to large caps. For our part, we will remain on the lookout for opportunities to use this environment to our advantage as we seek compelling investments that may be missed by other investors. We remain steadfast in our commitment to our core process of identifying well-managed and attractively valued companies with proven earnings growth, strong free cash flow, and low debt levels. We believe such companies will be positioned for outperformance regardless of the uncertain economic, geopolitical, and interest rate backdrop.

Portfolio management

Kevin PrelogerJustin Tugman, CFA

Definitions

Volatility measures risk using the dispersion of returns for a given investment.

Free cash flow (FCF) yield is a financial ratio that measures how much cash flow a company has in case of its liquidation or other obligations by comparing the free cash flow per share with the market price per share and indicates the level of cash flow the company will earn against its share market value.

Basis point (BP) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%.

10-Year Treasury Yield is the interest rate on U.S. Treasury bonds that will mature 10 years from the date of purchase.

Monetary Policy refers to the policies of a central bank, aimed at influencing the level of inflation and growth in an economy. It includes controlling interest rates and the supply of money.

To receive a complete list and description of composites and/or a presentation that complies with the requirements of the GIPS® standards, please contact Janus Henderson at 800.668.0434. The opinions are as of 12/31/24, are subject to change and may not reflect the views of others in the organization. Janus Henderson may have a business relationship with certain entities discussed. The comments should not be construed as a recommendation of individual holdings or market sectors, but as an illustration of broader themes.

Mid Cap Value Managed Account Composite, benchmarked to the Russell Midcap Value Index, includes portfolios that are broadly diversified and seek to identify quality mid-sized US companies trading at discounted prices with favorable risk/reward potential. Portfolios invest primarily in US companies whose market capitalization, at time of initial purchase, fall within the 12-month average of the capitalization range of the Russell Midcap Value Index. Prior to January 1, 2005 returns for the composite are for the Institutional Perkins Mid Cap Value Composite, which included institutional accounts and mutual funds prior to 2003. Prior to May 2021, the composite was known as the Perkins Mid Cap Value Managed Account Composite. The composite was created in January 2005.

Information relating to portfolio holdings is based on the representative account in the composite, which reflects the typical portfolio management style of the investment strategy. Other accounts in the strategy may vary due to asset size, client guidelines and other factors.

Portfolio holdings are as of the date indicated, and are subject to change. This material should not be construed as recommendation to buy or sell any security. Holdings are subject to change without notice.

For equity portfolios, relative contribution compares the performance of a security in the portfolio to the benchmark’s total return, factoring in the difference in weight of that security in the benchmark. Returns are calculated using daily returns and previous day ending weights rolled up by ticker, gross of advisory fees, may exclude certain derivatives and does not represent actual performance.

There is no assurance the stated objective(s) will be met.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal and fluctuation of value.

Discussion is based on performance gross of fees and expenses.

Smaller capitalization securities may be less stable and more susceptible to adverse developments, and may be more volatile and less liquid than larger capitalization securities.

Value stocks can continue to be undervalued by the market for long periods of time and may not appreciate to the extent expected.

Actively managed portfolios may fail to produce the intended results. No investment strategy can ensure a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Russell Midcap® Value Index reflects the performance of U.S. mid-cap equities with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values.

Index returns are provided to represent the investment environment existing during the periods shown. The index is fully invested, including the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Index returns do not include any transaction costs, management fees or other costs, and are gross of non-reclaimable withholding taxes, if any and unless otherwise noted.

Janus Henderson Investors claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®). For the purpose of claiming GIPS compliance, Janus Henderson Investors defines its GIPS Firm as the following entities within Janus Henderson Group plc that directly manage assets: Janus Henderson Investors UK Limited, Janus Henderson Investors (Singapore) Limited (excluding private equity assets), Janus Henderson Fund Management UK Limited, Janus Henderson Investors US LLC and Janus Henderson Investors (AuStralia) Institutional Funds Management Limited. The GIPS firm was formed on January 1, 2018 as a result of the merger of the predecessor GIPS firms Janus Capital Management LLC and Henderson Global Investors, which previously claimed compliance since January 1, 1994 and January 1, 2009, respectively.

Janus Henderson provides investment advisory services in the U.S. through Janus Henderson Investors US LLC, together with its participating affiliates. Janus Henderson is a trademark of Janus Henderson Group plc or one of its subsidiaries. © Janus Henderson Group plc.

GIPS® is a registered trademark of CFA Institute. CFA Institute does not endorse or promote this organization, nor does it warrant the accuracy or quality of the content contained herein.

data.title: Capital Power: Too Far, Too Fast (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Capital Power Corporation (OTCPK:CPXWF) is a North American independent power producer with ~10GW of gas and renewables fleet capacity across Canada and the US. The portfolio is, to a large extent, locked into long-term contracts, with most of the value coming from the US (4GW combined-cycle gas turbine or ‘CCGT’ fleet) and Alberta (Genesee 1/2/3 and Halkirk Wind, among other assets). The latter has been key to Capital Power’s re-rating over the last year, prompted by the promise of an AI-driven resurgence in the region.

Now, I've been bullish on Capital Power for some time now (see Capital Power: Risk/Reward Turns Increasingly Favorable Following Midland Cogen Acquisition) and the Alberta data center theme is certainly a legitimate one; but there are important caveats. With multiples also repriced quite significantly higher, even after the DeepSeek-driven dip and a relatively mixed 2025 update, I'm paring back my bullishness on the stock.

Puts and Takes from the Latest Guidance Update

Capital Power management was recently out with a 2025 “guidance presentation”, albeit with slightly below-par new targets for the year. For starters, adj. EBITDA is expected to land within a $1,340-$1,440m range (vs. $1,310-$1,410m in 2024e), while adj. Funds From Operations or ‘AFFO’ is set to come in at $850-$950m (vs. $770-$870m in 2024e).

Both 2025 midpoints ($1,390m adj EBITDA and $900m AFFO) were below consensus, despite benefiting from new asset contributions (full-year La Paloma, Harquahala, Halkirk 2, and Ontario BESS), as well as a modest cash tax benefit.

Detractors at the AFFO level were mainly higher interest expenses and “sustaining” capex ($195-$225m vs. $180-$200m in 2024e), while at the EBITDA level, the key negative was lower Alberta/California energy margins.

For income investors, the modest miss shouldn’t matter in theory, as Capital Power’s dividend remains on track for growth - both from a higher payout ratio (current 40% remains below the 45–55% target range) and cash generation. But Capital Power’s transition from ‘yield’ to ‘growth’ story over the last year means the stock is as sensitive as it’s ever been to growth expectations; hence, the need to also evaluate future growth.

Alberta and Data Centers Remain Top of Mind

The other big takeaway was Capital Power’s “strategic priorities” for the year, namely:

  • Contract Optimization
  • Optimize and Expand
  • Acquire
  • Develop

The focus here is really on re-contracting its US gas assets (La Paloma, Midland, Decatur Energy, etc.) at higher rates, as many of them approach contract expiry in the next five years.

But there is a data center angle here as well and rightly so, given Capital Power’s capacity in Alberta, currently in pole position to capture Canada’s data center demand. Of course, it remains early days, and nothing concrete has materialized just yet. But the structural advantages are hard to ignore, from Alberta's abundant natural gas to cold temperatures. Also, a very supportive administration, led by Technology and Innovation Minister Nate Glubish, who has targeted $100bn of data center investments by 2030.

Now, the potential upside here is massive, especially if data center demand actually sustains into the mid to longer term. Per management, US data center power demand alone is expected to grow at a ~17% annualized pace through 2030 - not only from AI, but also from cloud computing, video/streaming, and other tech applications. Put differently, it adds 0.9 percentage points per year to a status quo +2%/year power demand growth algorithm.

For now, the latest management estimates peg ~1.6GW of additional natural gas capacity through 2027 and ~4.7GW in 2028 for data center contracting, so Capital Power is well-positioned. The caveat is that no contracts have actually been announced yet in Alberta; nor is there anything concrete to underwrite outside of news flow and management commentary. And from a stock price perspective, data center contracts are already being priced in, so even if we do see announcements in the coming months, the next leg of multiple expansion likely won't come as easily.

Keep an Eye on the Funding

Besides the mid-term upside from re-contracting its US-based flexible generation assets and the longer-term ‘optionality’ from data centers, Capital Power’s dry powder means there’s an M&A angle to factor in the near-term as well. Recall that this is a serial acquirer, so with the company now “fully funded” for 2025 and “ready to grow”, expect additional growth M&A announcements in the coming months.

The caveat, though, is that even with ~$1.1bn of funding sources (AFFO, reinvested dividends, and cash/credit) and coming off a fresh $460m equity raise in December last year, there’s always the risk of more equity issuances here. And over the mid to longer term, balancing inorganic growth with the dividend commitment (+6% to 2025 and +2-4% beyond 2025), a key part of the annual 12-14% shareholder return target, will be tricky.

To be clear, I don’t necessarily see a dilution event in the very near-term. Especially if everything goes well, from incremental EBITDA contribution (Genesee, Harquahala, and La Paloma) coming through this year to the disposal pipeline ($340m raised from two Canadian wind facilities at good valuations). Nevertheless, more equity raises will be worth keeping an eye out for, especially with the stock now priced at re-rated multiples.

Summing Up

There’s no doubt Capital Power has a compelling growth trajectory ahead of it. The well-covered (and growing) ~5% dividend yield doesn’t hurt either. But at a premium ~21x forward earnings and ~1.7x book, it pays to be a little more cautious, in my view, particularly with quite a bit of data center optimism already priced in at this point.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Earnings Reactions And The Next Wave Of AI Speculation
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

CPI radiation and Washington shenanigans (0:30). Old school consumer stocks did well post-earnings (1:40). Tesla and perceived distractions (4:00). Palantir the next wave of AI speculation (5:30). Walmart earnings ahead; what will tariffs bring? (7:00)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our director of news here at Seeking Alpha, welcome back to our Friday conversations. Always great to have you.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here, Rena.

RS: So what are you looking at today? What are you fielding from the market? How are you tiering up the different levels of importance in the market?

BS: I think that there's a background radiation going on in terms of the CPI report that recently came out and the effect on the Fed. Also, shenanigans out of Washington, a lot of DOGE related headlines coming out recently.

I think that's going to be a standard background noise for trading in the foreseeable future. It seems that we're going to have to rifle through the bickering in Washington going forward.

However, I think the stock market itself has seemed pretty immune to any of that. There hasn't been a pickup in aggressive volatility over the last several weeks.

I think, by and large, it's been following momentum of its own. A lot of that is still the AI trade in effect. And I think there's still a lot of individual stock picking.

I think with the earnings season peaking in the last couple of weeks, I think investors have largely been looking at the individual company's financial results and not been too worried about the macro scene.

RS: So what are you seeing from those results? What has you most interested, either on the plus side or the minus side?

BS: One interesting theme over the last week is there's there's quite a few old school consumer companies that did well following their earnings.

One example is Philip Morris (PM), which jumped 14% in the past week. That was an individual company story. The company is seeing a very successful transition to smoke free products. And so moving out of the traditional tobacco cigarettes and moving into things like nicotine packages and vapes.

And so that process was seen as being aggressively underway in the earnings and so that drove earnings there.

But you also have companies like Coca Cola (KO), which beat expectations, showed strong organic sales growth.

There's worry still about higher costs and the prospects of tariffs affecting a large international company like Coke. But by and large, the results going into the current quarter that just finished have have shown that it's prospering pretty well.

And the same with a company like McDonald's (MCD), which is up 6% in the past week. Interestingly, it missed expectations, but investors tended to focus more on the strength in global sales that showed as well as the investments in value deals.

So companies like Coke and McDonald's I think are seen as value plays in an inflationary environment.

You have these strong brands that offer low cost products. There's a view that those kind of companies will prosper in that environment.

McDonald's missed expectations, but still went higher. Uber (UBER) dropped in the immediate aftermath of its earnings, but is up 23% in the past week as a whole.

So there was sort of a dip and then sort of a buy on the dip. I think the main catalyst there is Bill Ackman announced a stake in the company, and I think a lot of people saw that as a starter pistol for a higher move up. So I think there's a lot of traders that jumped into that on his coat tails to ride that trade higher.

Also, Lyft (LYFT) dropped after its earnings on a light booking forecast. So I think Uber saw the benefit of that. I think there was the idea that Uber might be winning that rivalry.

And in general - and this has been a theme of a lot of companies. I mean, Tesla (TSLA) is a great example of a company that has seen significant benefit on the prospects of future technology, especially autonomous vehicles.

And Uber is right there getting a tailwind from predictions that eventually it'll be able to stick the AV landing and be able to finally have that fuel the top and bottom lines going forward.

RS: In the AI conversation, a a conversation we love to take part in, and to a lesser degree that they're in the AI conversation, but certainly it was present this week in the conversation around OpenAI was Tesla and the converse, doing not so well this week. Care to contextualize the AI part of that and for investors what it means?

BS: Just to discuss Tesla briefly, I think that has a lot to do with the perceived distraction of Elon Musk being at the centerpiece of the DOGE debates, and also making a bid and then withdrawing a bid for OpenAI.

I think people did not see that as a serious attempt to take over that company and more of a publicity stunt. And so I think there's a sense to which he's taking his eye off the ball in terms of Tesla specifically.

And I also think that Tesla stock ran up significantly in the post election time frame, and there's a process of profit taking that's going on for that. And so it's down 11% in the past week and is at a three month low.

But like I said, I think that's largely Musk personality related and not really a statement about people's opinions about AI or even Tesla's underpinning prospects.

If we look at Palantir (PLTR), I think that's an interesting one because it rallied immediately after its earnings report, had a blockbuster financial figures, and has continued to stair step higher since.

It's been alternating up days and down days since its earnings, but the up days are all bigger than the down days. So it's an aggressive buy and dip going on there.

Palantir has become the symbol of the next wave of AI speculation. Wedbush said about its earnings, the AI software era, is now arriving. I'm paraphrasing there. So the idea that the value of AI in the first wave, it was the NVIDIAs (NVDA) of the world and the pickaxe and shovel type companies for this gold rush.

And now we're moving into the companies that are actually putting it into effect in these products and the productivity and upgrades that it can give to customer UX based on AI.

And so I think Palantir has become the spotlight company for that, and I think it's getting a lot of the money that's come out of some of the chipmakers and the other first wave AI winners. Some of that money that's being trimmed out of those is moving into Palantir.

RS: Anything else to say before we start another week or another weekend as the case may be?

BS: Just looking ahead to next week, the earnings calendar is starting to slow down. There's still a fair number of reports coming out, but the biggest names are off the table at this point.

If you're looking for a highlight for next week I think Walmart (WMT) is gonna get a lot of attention. It's sitting at fifty two week highs recently. It's up 83% in the past twelve months. So in a lot of ways, it's been trading like a growth stock even though there couldn't be a more stable stock.

RS: I was gonna say, speaking of value stocks.

BS: Yeah. Right. But, yeah, there's been a rush into Walmart. It was not even a rush. It's sort of a steady drip, drip, drip over the last twelve months.

And I think that's partly the same kind of Coke, McDonald's value play. The expectation that inflation is going to drive people to lower price alternatives. I think Walmart's also getting a lot of credit for its ecommerce gains.

It's making a lot of inroads there and in international expansion. One thing to look out for when the earnings come out is whether or not the company is worried about tariffs.

Again, it's a large international company. Its low price strategy is based around getting cheap goods from overseas. And so if the management there is starting to sweat a little bit about potential tariffs, I think that would be a sign that other companies might be following in those footsteps.

RS: And how would they convey that - straight up or code it in some kind of language?

BS: Yeah. I would expect it to emerge in the earnings conference call. I don't expect in the press release they'll say anything about it unless they actually temper financial expectations. Sales growth won't be as strong as we thought because, tariffs.

But I don't expect that to happen. I think it'll be more like you're saying, coded. And I think it'll come out in the q and a in the earnings report.

RS: You think the question will be asked and they'll have to address whether or not it's a concern of theirs?

BS: Honestly, I would be surprised if an analyst doesn't ask about tariffs in the conference call then I don't know that they're doing their job effectively. I think if you're a Walmart investor,I can't imagine anything else being on your mind at this point.

RS: When does Walmart report?

BS: On Thursday next week.

data.title: AFK: So Contrarian, It Just Might Work (New Buy Rating)
data.description:
I am a firm believer that among the biggest misjudgments investors make regarding ETFs are these:

  • They pay too much attention to past performance. There's only one guarantee there...that you can't have that past performance. Not if you are just looking it the ETF or stock for the first time now.
  • They assign too much importance to an ETF's expense ratio. Lower "ER" is better, but if there's a good reason it is higher, I don't care if the ER is 1% or even 1.5%. You often get what you pay for. Funds that saved investors' retirements via hedging techniques were worth the slightly higher imbedded fees. I would pay an extra 1% any time, if I believed it would limit a 20% drop in portfolio value to a small fraction of that, or even to make money in a bear market. That's actually my investing career in a nutshell. It is also why the investing group I run here is not the least expensive. I play defense, not just offense, and teach my subscribers to that in whatever way they see fit.
  • They don't look through to the ETFs holdings to get a better sense of what they truly own. And they gravitate to ETFs that sound different from others they have, but effectively are not. I call that a "collection of ETFs" and not a coordinated portfolio.

These keys to ETF investing (my keys, that is, among others), are all front and center in my thesis for placing an initial buy rating on the VanEck Africa Index ETF (NYSEARCA:AFK).

Africa? Really? What on earth is that about?

It is about evolving and adapting to the markets we have, not the markets we wish we had. I wish the S&P 500 and the US market overall were not so hyper-focused on a couple of handfuls of stocks. I wish meme stocks were not 100x more valuable than businesses built, grown and maintained for 150 years. And I wish the US Dollar would finally reflect the historic risk level that is implied by the massive amount of US government debt, built up by both sides of the aisle.

But I can't do anything about those things. What I can do it expand my field of vision. In the case of AFK, it is the only Africa stock pure play in the ETF world. There's a South Africa single country ETF, but that's a small segment of the continent's potential, something I am starting to learn a lot about this year. I was asked to get involved in a non-profit project that is helping thousands of African children take their love of baseball (who knew?) and provide resources and organization. I'm a baseball fan for life, and I have family in Africa, so I didn't need much of an excuse to do some research on this unique ETF. I've known about it for years, but never devoted much attention to it.

And, spoiler alert, I plan to buy AFK in my portfolio soon. So it will get a "speculative long-term buy" rating, if you will. But as with a bunch of other high risk/high reward potential ETFs and stocks I'm researching, hoping to identify maybe 20-30 of them, the position size commitment I plan to make is very, very small. Figure 1% of one of my smaller accounts. Just enough to track them as a group, and like a private equity manager with public market liquidity, I hope that over the next few years, enough will be "flyers" for me that the whole venture within that part of my portfolio will be wildly profitable.

So, that's the context in which I scouted AFK. Now to the analysis.

So many reasons not to buy AFK, yet I'm doing just that

Has it performed well? What's the opposite of that! AFK has been an undeniable disaster. But the past means nothing when this little ETF's entire existence has been an era of US stock outperformance. All it would take is a reversal in the US dollar (south) and the corresponding sentiment could create some lift here. If you believe as I do that 600% performance gaps over nearly 2 decades are made to be narrowed, at least a bit, then AFK is at least on the radar.

I can't fault the algorithm that could not find a reasonable peer group for AFK. There really isn't one. And I am all for uniqueness in this brave new investing world we are in.

The Expense ratio is over 1%. I don't care. I'm not in this to try to make a little more than SPY or QQQ. I'm in it for diversification and low correlation to US markets (obviously, since it has never kept up with SPY, noted above). And, for the big wildcard: liquidity flows!

What does that mean? For a quick example, how did bitcoin rise so much in price? How did 7 magnificent companies become magnificent giant stocks? Liquidity. Sure, it starts with a business case, but that alone doesn't raise stock prices. Buying interest does. And as the only one of its kind, AFK at under $45mm in AUM and very low volume is just big enough for me to dip a toe in the water.

And if at some point, some segment of the global markets decide Africa's eternal growth potential is finally starting to be realized, that's all it will take for the underlying stocks, markets and AFK to create a lot of alpha. Worst case for me, it's a small position, and I've lost more on SPY options that went the wrong way!

A key to liquidity at the ETF level is the bid/ask spread, more so than the trading volume of the ETF. If the market makers can make a market, shares can be bought in some size. Still, I would not for a moment assume liquidity here will be normal versus most ETFs. But it will be more liquid than private debt, real estate and other asset types that I might consider for part of my smaller, aggressive long-term portfolio I'm building as part of the Sungarden YARP Portfolio investing group.

There's an interesting clue below. I don't often refer to Seeking Alpha ETF quant grades, though I do use their stock grades a lot. But that momentum at A- is hard to find these days. Many stocks I see are C and D ratings. So I noted that in my research.

And here is the best reason to "take a small position and look to the future" with AFK. The chart below shows me that while there may be some traffic immediately ahead, this ETF has a chance to break through meaningfully, long-term.

And while performance has been terribly long-term, my first objective with any investment is to make 10%, then go from there. Put another away, a stock can't double unless it goes up 10% first.

Those are my standard quantitative and technical testing points that I use for nearly any ETF I consider. Now, let's finish up with the holdings' breakdown.

The fund's issuer is solid. Jan VanEck's firm is now into its second generation of leadership, and they have built one of the more meaningful ETF product complexes. They are far from the biggest, but they are not afraid to start and continue with ETFs that seek alpha in uncommon places. That said, is there a possibility they could close this fund down, liquidate its holdings and call it a day on AFK? Of course there is. But I would not anticipate that event, if it did occur, being too disruptive. Hundreds of ETFs open and close every year.

The fund's website describes AFK this way:

VanEck Africa Index ETF (AFK®) seeks to replicate as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the price and yield performance of the MVIS® GDP Africa Index (MVAFKTR), which includes local listings of companies that are incorporated in Africa and listings of companies incorporated outside of Africa, but that have at least 50% of their revenues/related assets in Africa.

So there are pure Africa stocks and others that do most of their business there. This is how the ETF's country allocation looks. South Africa is the natural hub, given that market is the most advanced on the continent by far.

AFK yields 3%, which is a nice bonus, but far down my list of reasons to own it. I can get 3% in many places. The fund holds under 40 stocks, which I like a lot. This will likely prompt me to do some follow-on research on some of the top holdings to see if a few should be on my stock research list for this same long-term aggressive portfolio I decided to run for myself, and add as another feature of the new investing group service.

That PE Ratio!

Before we leave the table above, here's the kicker for me on AFK. 10.4x trailing earnings. 1.1x trailing sales. That PE is 1/3 cheaper than small cap US stocks, which are fraught with "going concern" risk since their debt levels depend on lower interest rates for survival. And they might not get them in time.

AFK's weighted average market cap is around $7.4 billion, just 20% below the $8.8 billion figure for US midcaps and $2.8 billion for US small caps. So these are not fledging companies in AFK. They just feed the needs of businesses and consumers on the African continent. And I'm not taking single stock risk here. It is an ETF stock basket.

And that basket is growing earnings, as shown here.

Technology stocks are not a part of AFK, so that's a great diversifying accolade here. Materials stock are a high sector weighting, so AFK might have some inflation-friendly characteristics versus the standard US big cap fare. Communications is another growth area of note, since this is mostly the developing or even frontier world we are talking about.

For clarification, frontier economies are even less developed than emerging markets. Africa has a lot of both, creating a potential long-term "discovery" situation. I'd note also that on this continent, for millions the first phone they ever had was a mobile phone.

Note that the South African gold miners US investors are familiar with are well-represented here. But they don't dominate the portfolio, so I don't consider AFK to be some sort of closet gold play.

Below are the top 25 holdings, most of the ETF's assets. Lots of losers the past year. That's good since I didn't own it, and comeback stories are what investing is all about...to us contrarians.

AFK: a speculative long-term buy for me

I had a friend who used to like to say about high-risk investments, "hey, I've lost that amount of money on dumber ideas than this!" We would laugh then, but the serious part here is that my AFK buy is about testing the waters. This is not like when I buy a blue-chip stock, even with an option collar around it, which I am prone to doing these days.

This is one that has a chance, based on a combination of the chart, the depressed valuation, and the "home run" potential, of being discovered, finally. I'm buying AFK in small size, and will look to follow up on this intriguing ETF down the road.

data.title: Micron: This Stock Won't Be This Cheap For Much Longer
data.description:
Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) shares have been quite volatile the last couple months since a lackluster earnings report and some upheaval in the AI sector courtesy of DeepSeek. The stock is still trading at fairly depressed levels relative to most of 2024 and I think investors should continue to view this as a buying opportunity. Improving consumer demand, ongoing strength in the data center, and the ramp of HBM position MU as an attractive target.

As many might recall, we're a couple months removed from what I would call a disappointing earnings report from Micron as weak consumer demand weighed on margins in Q1 and the company projected further NAND weakness into Q2. In the wake of that report, I penned a piece rating the stock a Strong Buy in anticipation of a 2H2025 recovery in consumer demand paired with expected strength in leading-edge DRAM like DDR5 and HBM. That article can be read here.

In the time since, MU has risen more than 20% back to $110 per share and is now back around the same price it was post-earnings. Much of this movement mirrors the broader AI sector with highs in late January followed by a DeepSeek-fueled sell-off. More on DeepSeek later.

So after the ups and downs and with the new information we've received, is MU still positioned for success? Micron's CFO, Mark Murphy, spoke Wednesday at a conference, discussing some of the ongoing developments in the memory market that might help us answer this question.

Murphy's opening remarks maintained Micron's guidance for Q2, but did provide some generalized commentary on expectations for Q3 that I found interesting. Namely, as I expected, the company now expects the consumer market to pick up in the quarter due to increased demand for smartphones and PCs and normalizing inventory levels. As this was one of the factors that led to Micron's poor Q2 projections, it's encouraging to see this market rebound.

However, as a side effect of the consumer recovery, Micron expects Q3 margins to decline by "a few 100 basis points" sequentially from the 38.5% gross margin expected for Q2 since these consumer products are lower margin than, say, enterprise SSDs. Also weighing on margins is the ongoing weakness in the NAND market, which will persist into Q3, coupled with under-utilization of NAND fab capacity in an attempt to align supply with demand.

Despite the margin hit, I think this scenario is fairly bullish for Micron. A recovery in consumer sales and the contribution to revenue will provide higher overall earnings even if the sales mix technically brings down gross margin. As for NAND, Murphy stated that they expect Q4 to show signs of recovery for NAND as excess supply flushes from the channel, providing some pricing relief. He also sent a not-so-subtle message to other NAND suppliers that scaling back would be in the industry's best interest:

Well, there are some encouraging signs, but this is one of these things that there needs to be continued discipline. There needs to be a continued drawdown of inventories in NAND, and that’s happening.

CEO Sanjay Mehrotra adopted a similar tone in the Q1 report as he announced Micron had aggressively pared back NAND capital expenditures in a signal for Samsung and SK Hynix to do the same. At 26% of Micron's revenue, NAND remains a vital piece of the puzzle and I think it's recovery from these levels of oversupply will help boost operating results in the second half of the calendar year.

On the DRAM side, the sentiment was largely the same as in the Q1 report -- which is to say the news is all good. Supply for DDR5 and HBM remains tight and Murphy said the company expects that to continue to be the case throughout 2025. The hyperscalers have committed tens of billions of dollars to infrastructure build-outs, which require ample supply of DDR5, HBM, and enterprise NAND SSD. The demand for these products will drive a return to revenue growth in the second half of the year, especially HBM.

Supply remains extremely tight for HBM as Micron's capacity for the entire FY2025 is already sold out. The higher margins on these sales should offset some of the margin pressure from a consumer-heavy mix in Q3 and will, I expect, push margins back into the 40%+ range in the second half of 2025. As far as market share goes, there was an interesting exchange regarding Samsung's struggles getting HBM yields up to desired levels. After being asked how the issues at Samsung factor into Micron's goal of achieving 20% market share in the HBM market, Murphy stated:

We do believe that Samsung will be successful. That’s our base case. And so, we will with our superior product capabilities, technology capabilities, we would expect to be able to achieve our target DRAM share, our target HBM share.

So if Samsung joins the party, Micron expects to gain 20% share anyway, but if Samsung should continue to fail, well that's more of the pie for everyone else!

Since it was all the rage a couple weeks ago, let's also discuss the implications of the DeepSeek news that sent ripples through the AI market. I've written a few articles mentioning the likely fallout of DeepSeek's breakthrough and my overall takeaway is that, if the company's claims are true, model training is at risk of becoming commoditized; if companies don't need Nvidia's (NVDA) latest GPUs to train their LLMs, then that hurts the pricing power of GPUs for training applications, which is Nvidia's monopoly to lose.

However, this has the potential to proportionately increase the need for inference infrastructure (i.e. more data center SSDs) as the demand for serving interactions with these cheaper training models will increase as well. Additionally, AMD (AMD) and Nvidia are poised to release bigger and badder GPUs in 2025, including AMD's MI355X, which will have 288 GB of HBM3E, and Nvidia's GB200, which will have 192 GB, demonstrating the increasing demand for these high-margin products in the processors of the future.

Lastly on the DRAM side, as expected, DDR4 remains under pressure from a supply standpoint as Chinese memory producers continue to press the gas pedal, but as Micron mentioned in the last earnings call, the low-end market is becoming an ever-smaller piece of Micron's mix and isn't all that relevant from an investing perspective.

Investor Takeaway

My overall assessment here is that MU is probably close to a relative trough in price as consumer and NAND pressures come to a head in Q3. Now, you might be thinking "well if Q3 will be the worst why should I buy before then!?" Because the market is forward looking, MU tends to inflect around the time the company's operating results are approaching a bottom (or a top). Considering Micron's optimism for a NAND recovery in Q4, consumer recovery in Q3, and continued DRAM outperformance, I think the second half of the year has the potential for Micron to be firing on all cylinders.

Though margins could remain ugly for a couple quarters, the path forward for Micron is looking up and current prices appear attractive; therefore I am re-iterating a Strong Buy on MU.

Thanks for reading and best of luck!
